title: Burchard of Worms ranks same-sex acts as ordinary sexual sins
description: In his canon-law collection Decretum, Bishop Burchard of Worms classes homosexual acts alongside transgressions such as adultery, concluding they deserve the same penance, typically fasting.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Peter Damian demands harsher church penalties in Liber Gomorrhianus
description: The monk Peter Damian completes the treatise Liber Gomorrhianus, urging stricter punishment of clerics he accuses of failing in their duty against so-called vices of nature.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Two men wed by a priest in the Kingdom of Leon
description: A priest marries Pedro Dias and Muño Vandilas at a chapel in the Kingdom of Leon, in present-day Spain — one of the earliest recorded church weddings of two men.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Pope Urban II declines to act against Bishop Giovanni of Orleans
description: Ivo of Chartres accuses Archbishop Rodolfo of Tours of arranging the appointment of his lover Giovanni as bishop of Orleans, a liaison the French king openly acknowledged sharing. Pope Urban II takes no action; Giovanni holds the see for nearly forty years while Rodolfo remains a respected figure.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Council of London declares homosexuality sinful
description: A church council in London takes steps to make clear to the English public that homosexuality counts as sin.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Council of Nablus prescribes burning for sodomy
description: King Baldwin II of the crusader Kingdom of Jerusalem convenes the Council of Nablus, which rules that people who repeatedly commit sodomy are to be burned.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Gratian brands sodomy the gravest sexual sin
description: The monk Gratian compiles the canon-law collection Concordia discordantium canonum, arguing that sodomy outranks all other sexual sins because it puts the body to an unnatural use.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Aelred of Rievaulx writes On Spiritual Friendship
description: The English monk Aelred of Rievaulx completes De spiritali amicitia, a work that gives deep expression to love between people of the same gender.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Third Lateran Council orders excommunication of sodomites
description: The Third Lateran Council, meeting in Rome, issues a decree excommunicating people found guilty of sodomy — church-wide legislation with effect across Latin Christendom.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Inquisition begins pursuing sodomy in the Italian city-states
description: Pope Gregory IX launches the Inquisition in the Italian city-states. Several cities punish first and second sodomy convictions with banishment or amputation, and habitual or third-time offenders with burning.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: France imposes mutilation then death for same-sex acts
description: French law of this period punishes a first sodomy conviction with castration, a second with further mutilation, and a third with burning. Women convicted of same-sex acts likewise face mutilation or execution.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Thomas Aquinas ranks sodomy just below bestiality among sins of lust
description: In his theological writing, Thomas Aquinas argues that among the sins of lust only bestiality is worse than sodomy — a ranking that shaped centuries of Catholic moral teaching.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Coutumes de Beauvaisis adds property forfeiture to burning for sodomy
description: The French legal compilation Coutumes de Beauvaisis states that convicted sodomites are to be burned and that their property falls to the crown.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Philip IV destroys the Templars using sodomy charges
description: King Philip IV of France has the Knights Templar arrested on charges of heresy, idolatry and sodomy, a pretext for seizing the order's wealth. The order's leaders are burned at the stake near Notre-Dame in Paris on 18 March 1314.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Dante's Inferno consigns sodomites to the Seventh Circle
description: Dante Alighieri's Inferno places sodomites in the Seventh Circle of Hell, fixing the association between same-sex acts and damnation in one of the most influential works of European literature.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Deposed King Edward II of England is killed
description: Edward II, deposed as king of England, is put to death — by later accounts with a heated poker forced into his rectum. His reign had been marked by conflict with nobles who repeatedly exiled his former lover Piers Gaveston, Earl of Cornwall.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Rolandino Roncaglia tried for sodomy in Italy
description: The trial of Rolandino Roncaglia causes a sensation in Italy. Roncaglia testified to having no desire for women, lived and worked as a woman after being widowed, and described being widely taken for one — a rare documented case of gender nonconformity in medieval court records.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Thai temple murals depict same-sex relationships
description: Buddhist temple murals showing same-sex relationships are commissioned and painted in Thailand.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: King Gongmin, known for male lovers, takes the Goryeo throne
description: Gongmin, recorded in Korean sources as having male lovers, becomes king of Goryeo in Korea.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Jan van Aersdone and Willem Case executed in Antwerp
description: During the 1370s, Jan van Aersdone and Willem Case are executed in Antwerp for same-sex intercourse, then illegal across medieval Europe. Their case is among the few from the era where the names of the accused survive, alongside Giovanni Braganza and Nicoleto Marmagna of Venice.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: John Rykener arrested in London for cross-dressing
description: John Rykener, also called Eleanor, a sex worker active around Cheapside in London and in Oxford, is arrested for cross-dressing and interrogated — one of the few surviving medieval English records touching on gender nonconformity and sex work.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Bernardino of Siena preaches against homosexuality in Florence
description: Bernardino of Siena spends three days preaching in Florence against homosexuality and other perceived vices, ending with a bonfire of cosmetics and finery. He calls for sodomites to be cast out of society; his sermons harden public opinion and push the authorities toward tougher persecution.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Texcoco makes homosexuality a capital offense
description: Nezahualcoyotl, ruler of Texcoco in the Valley of Mexico, enacts laws punishing homosexuality with death by hanging.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Florence creates the Night Officials to prosecute sodomy
description: Florence establishes the Night Officials, the first body created specifically to prosecute sodomy. Over the following seventy years the office arrests around 10,000 men and boys and secures roughly 2,000 convictions, most ending in fines.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Royal Noble Consort Sun banished from the Joseon court
description: Royal Noble Consort Sun is expelled from the Joseon court in Korea after her relationship with her maid comes to light. The official decree instead cites receiving visitors without her husband's permission and having her maids sing men's songs.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Pope Nicholas V authorizes the Inquisition to pursue sodomy
description: Pope Nicholas V empowers the papal Inquisition to persecute men who practice sodomy.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Inca ruler Topa Inca Yupanqui persecutes homosexuals
description: According to Garcilaso de la Vega's chronicle of the Incas, Sapa Inca Topa Inca Yupanqui persecutes homosexuals during his reign. His general Auqui Tatu burns people alive in the Acari valley on even circumstantial evidence of sodomy, while in Chincha the ruler orders mass burnings and the destruction of homes and trees.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Chronicle records burnings of homosexuals in Peru
description: A chronicle written under the Capac Yupanqui government in Peru describes homosexuals being publicly burned and their homes destroyed, a treatment otherwise reserved for conquered tribes.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Leonardo da Vinci twice charged with sodomy and acquitted
description: Florentine court records show Leonardo da Vinci and three other young men facing sodomy charges twice in 1476; both cases end in acquittal.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Spanish Inquisition begins prosecuting sodomy
description: The Spanish Inquisition gets under way, punishing sodomy with stoning, castration and burning. Between 1540 and 1700 more than 1,600 people are prosecuted on sodomy charges.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Erasmus writes love letters to a fellow monk
description: While living at the monastery of Steyn in the Netherlands, Desiderius Erasmus writes a series of love letters to another monk.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Savonarola rails against homosexuality in Florence
description: Girolamo Savonarola berates the people of Florence for what he calls their horrible sins, chiefly homosexuality and gambling, urging them to abandon their young male lovers.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Spain extends and hardens its sodomy laws
description: Ferdinand of Aragon and Isabella of Castile extend sodomy laws previously limited to the cities across Spain. The offense is elevated to the level of treason or heresy, the evidence needed for conviction is lowered, torture is allowed to extract confessions, and the property of the convicted is confiscated.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Inca ruler Huayna Capac tolerates open same-sex practice
description: Garcilaso de la Vega's chronicle relates that during his reign Sapa Inca Huayna Capac merely asked the people of Tumbez to give up sodomy and took no measures against the Matna, described as practicing it more openly than any other tribe.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Sandro Botticelli accused of sodomy
description: A sodomy charge is brought against the Florentine painter Sandro Botticelli.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Zhengde Emperor linked to a same-sex relationship
description: The Zhengde Emperor of Ming-dynasty China is said to have had a same-sex relationship with the Muslim leader Sayyid Husain during his reign, though Chinese sources contain no evidence supporting the claim.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Compagnacci faction revolts in Florence
description: The Compagnacci, the Florentine faction that had opposed Savonarola's moral crackdown — including its campaign against sodomy — stage a revolt in Florence.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Balboa has forty Indigenous men killed as homosexuals
description: The conquistador Vasco Nunez de Balboa, in present-day Panama, is described as throwing forty Indigenous men accused of homosexuality to his dogs.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Magellan imposes the death penalty on crew over a same-sex relationship
description: Ferdinand Magellan sentences members of his own crew to death at Rio de Janeiro after judging them to have had a homosexual relationship.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Benvenuto Cellini first charged with sodomy
description: The Florentine artist Benvenuto Cellini faces the first of several sodomy charges brought against him over his lifetime.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Babur, first Mughal emperor, remembered for his love of Baburi Andijani
description: Babur establishes the Mughal Empire in South Asia. His memoirs record his long attachment to Baburi Andijani, a young man he loved before rising to power.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Holy Roman Empire makes sodomy a capital crime
description: The Holy Roman Empire adopts law making sodomy punishable by death, extending capital punishment for same-sex acts across its territories in central Europe.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Michelangelo begins his love poems to Tommaso dei Cavalieri
description: The Florentine artist Michelangelo starts writing what grows to more than 300 love poems dedicated to Tommaso dei Cavalieri.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history (CC BY-SA 4.0)
